claim_chain: Cortico-respiratory coupling exists in adults and is linked to diverse perceptual, cognitive, and motor processes
We demonstrate that cortico-respiratory coupling is present in premature and term newborns
Coupling is strongest during inspiration and cortical activity precedes respiration
Coupling is strongest over frontocentral regions, suggesting involvement of cortical motor areas
Cortico-respiratory coupling is negatively correlated with the rate of apnoea
These findings provide novel insight into neonatal apnoea pathology

typical_pattern: State what was done or measured
Present the key finding or observation
Provide statistical support or methodological detail
Interpret the finding in context of existing knowledge

likely_priorities: Spatial resolution limitations of the EEG approach
Causality determination between cortical activity and respiration
likely_attacks: Limited spatial resolution prevents definitive localization of cortical sources
Directionality analysis may not establish true causality
Sample heterogeneity across postmenstrual ages may confound results
Mechanism linking cortical activity to apnoea reduction is not fully explained
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly acknowledges 'Whilst our study was limited in spatial resolution, and determining causality is challenging' in the abstract. This self-identified limitation suggests the authors anticipate reviewer concerns about spatial localization and causal inference. The phrase 'we believe these findings support the notion' indicates hedging around mechanistic interpretation. The discussion contextualizes findings within adult literature, suggesting reviewers will compare infant and adult networks. The negative correlation with apnoea is presented as 'novel insight' but without full mechanistic explanation, suggesting this gap may be questioned.

examples.quoted_phrase: we believe these findings support the notion that the cortico-respiratory coupling observed here constitutes communication between cortical motor areas and lung effectors
We show that cortico-respiratory coupling is present in premature and term newborns
This indicates that the strength of cortico-respiratory coupling changes throughout the respiratory cycle
this suggests that cortical areas centred around the vertex may play an important role in the neural activation of respiratory muscles
These findings confirm that cortico-respiratory coupling occurs in infants

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, author names, or invented numbers were included. The profile is based solely on the provided abstract, discussion, and figure legends.
